title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the pioneer of nursing, Florence Nightingale. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: The Lady with the Lamp: A Statistical Visionary
content.text: Florence Nightingale is a household name, immortalized as the compassionate 'Lady with the Lamp' who tended to wounded soldiers during the Crimean War. However, this romanticized image often overshadows her true contribution to modern medicine. Nightingale was not merely a dedicated nurse; she was a brilliant polymath and a pioneer in the field of applied statistics. ||(41)|| [41]Her childhood was marked by an unusual education for a woman of the Victorian era[/41]. While her peers were taught social graces, Florence’s father provided her with a rigorous grounding in mathematics, history, and philosophy. This analytical foundation would later prove essential in her battle against the bureaucratic incompetence of the British military. ||(42)|| [42]Upon arrival at the Scutari hospital, she was horrified by the conditions[/42]. The facility sat atop a cesspool, and the soldiers were dying not from their battle wounds, but from preventable diseases like cholera and typhus. Most officials viewed these deaths as an inevitable consequence of war. ||(43)|| [43]To prove her point, she began meticulously collecting data on mortality rates[/43]. She realized that numbers alone were often dry and unpersuasive to the politicians in London. To solve this, she invented the 'polar area diagram,' a predecessor to the modern pie chart, which visually demonstrated that the vast majority of deaths were caused by poor sanitation. ||(44)|| [44]The impact of this visual evidence was immediate and profound[/44]. The government was forced to act, sending a sanitary commission to flush out the sewers and improve ventilation. Within months, the mortality rate at Scutari plummeted from 42% to just 2%. Nightingale had used mathematics to save more lives than any surgeon could with a scalpel. ||(45)|| [45]This institution was the first of its kind, transforming nursing from a lowly, disorganized occupation into a respected and scientifically-minded profession[/45]. Her curriculum emphasized both the technical skills of medical care and the importance of hospital administration. ||(46)|| [46]Even in her later years, as an invalid confined to her bed, she continued to advise governments on rural hygiene and army health[/46]. Her legacy is found not just in the hospitals we visit today, but in every data-driven decision made by public health officials. She proved that compassion, when coupled with cold, hard data, is the most powerful force for change.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, it was [41] her obsession with data [/41] that allowed her to revolutionize hospital management. She believed that social phenomena could be objectively measured and analyzed, a radical idea at a time when medical treatment was largely based on tradition and guesswork.
paragraphs.B: She refused to accept [43] this fatalistic outlook [/43]. Nightingale understood that without a radical change in hygiene, the British army would be decimated before it ever reached the front lines. She knew she needed more than just anecdotes to convince the authorities; she needed proof.
paragraphs.C: This [42] early exposure to logic [/42] gave her the tools to challenge the status quo. When the call came for nurses to go to the Crimea in 1854, she was uniquely prepared to manage the logistical nightmare that awaited her at the base hospital.
paragraphs.D: Following her [44] return to England as a national hero [/44], she used her influence and the funds raised by the public to establish the Nightingale Training School at St. Thomas' Hospital in London.
paragraphs.E: These [45] educational standards [/45] soon spread across the globe. By the end of the century, 'Nightingale nurses' were in high demand from New York to Sydney, ensuring that her principles of cleanliness and order became the global benchmark for patient care.
paragraphs.F: Her [46] tireless advocacy [/46] for reform did not end with nursing education. She became a consultant on the health of the British army in India, using her statistical skills to show that bad drainage and water supply were killing soldiers in peacetime as well as war.
paragraphs.G: Despite her [47] fame and achievements [/47], Nightingale remained a deeply private person. She often turned down public appearances and preferred to conduct her work through letters and reports, fearing that her celebrity status would distract from the urgent need for medical reform.

questions.explanation: A connects Nightingale's 'obsession with data' to her role as a 'pioneer in applied statistics' mentioned in the introduction.
C refers to her 'early exposure to logic', which links back to her 'rigorous grounding in mathematics' provided by her father.
B refers to 'this fatalistic outlook', which describes the officials' view that soldier deaths were 'inevitable' mentioned before the gap.
D mentions her 'return to England', following her success in Crimea, and introduces the 'Training School' mentioned in the next paragraph.
E refers to the 'educational standards' of the training school, which explains how the profession became respected worldwide.
F connects her 'tireless advocacy' and continued work in India to the description of her 'later years' in the final paragraph.
